subject: Polearms (spears, pikes, glaives, halberds)
method: full human/LLM read of transcript 064 (not the heuristic)
note: All 32 tiers below were confirmed to match the stored tierlists.json exactly. The 'why' field is new content. One of the strongest weapon categories (8 S-tier); rated by power when acquired. Pikes/glaives/halberds are two-handed reach (8ft); spears are versatile + thrown. Brace and Piercing Strike are the prized weapon actions.

Lightning Jabber.why: A +1 spear that applies shocking grasp (no reactions) and +1d4 lightning when thrown; on honour mode it's the best Act 1 throwing weapon for an Eldritch Knight (who can recall it), beating the Returning Pike on damage. (Insanely buggy/broken below honour mode.)

Returning Pike.why: A +1 pike that returns when thrown — best-in-slot for any non-Eldritch-Knight throwing character (probably the best strategy in the game), and a fine early melee pike with great weapon actions besides.

Sorrow.why: A +1 glaive granting at-will bonus-action Sorrowful Lash (a Thorn Whip that adds your spellcasting modifier and pulls enemies 10ft) — pickpocketable at the very start, so an early +1 glaive with a powerful repositioning bonus-action is S-tier.

The Skinburster.why: A +1 halberd granting 2 stacks of Force Conduit on damage (up to -7 physical damage taken, with AOE force retaliation at 5+); its Cleave stacks it fast (6 in one action), making the wielder nearly unkillable — superb on tiger-heart barbarians.

Unseen Menace.why: An invisible +1 pike granting advantage on every attack (until you miss) plus crits on 19-20 — the first source of permanent passive advantage; best-in-slot for early paladins (more crit smites) and Sentinel reach users.

Drakethroat Glaive.why: A +2 glaive whose Draconic Elemental Weapon can be cast on ANY weapon (a free Elemental Weapon: +1 enhancement and +1d4 elemental, all day) — it passively upgrades your whole party's best weapons from the inventory; arguably the best weapon in the game.

Halberd of Vigilance.why: A +2 halberd with +1d4 force, +1 initiative, advantage on perception, and advantage on reaction attacks — best-in-slot two-handed weapon when you get it, and a Drakethroat enchant turns it into a +3 with 2d4 bonus damage.

Shar's Spear of Evening.why: A +3 spear granting advantage on all saves while obscured, +1d6 vs obscured enemies, blind immunity (devil-sight-like darkness abuse), at-will Darkness, and an AOE darkness weapon action — possibly the single best weapon in the game.

Monster Slayer Glaive.why: A +1 glaive with +1d4 attack/damage vs monstrosities and +5ft jump; the bonuses are passive, so an archer can wield it in the unequipped slot for a huge boost (esp. dual hand crossbows) during monstrosity fights while keeping their real bow.

Vision of the Absolute.why: A no-enhancement spear: hits force a DC11 dex save (a weak save) or Blind, plus +2d6 vs multi-eyed enemies (spiders/spectators); the free on-hit blind is decent value and it's excellent for the early spider fight, though the low DC limits it.

Selune's Spear of Night.why: A legendary +3 spear, but mostly carried for its once/day Moonmote (60ft aura: +1d4 radiant to allied melee, difficult terrain for enemies, and free reverb/radiating-orb application); great for one encounter a day, unexciting otherwise — swap it out.

Hellbeard Halberd.why: A +2 halberd dealing a flat +6 poison and applying Poisoned (no save) on every hit — disadvantage on enemy attacks all fight, great for long boss fights vs non-poison-immune enemies and the poisoner item set; low end of A.

The Dancing Breeze.why: A +2 glaive that's also a finesse weapon with a once/rest Whirlwind AOE — lets a Dexterity character use Polearm Master/Sentinel reach builds with the best stat in the game, enabling many builds (e.g. Sentinel paladin) that otherwise wouldn't work.

+1 Spear.why: A +1 thrown weapon — a better emergency/initiating throw than the non-magical version (+1 damage thrown), worth stockpiling for a throwing-weapon character, though hand axes/javelins are usually preferred for weight.

The Watcher's Guide.why: A no-enhancement spear granting True Strike (advantage) on your next attack after a miss; often the first magical weapon you find, so worth picking up and using for an encounter or two before swapping.

Moonlight Glaive.why: A +2 glaive with +1d4 radiant and a once/rest Moonlight Butterflies attack (≈+8 damage, advantage aura); a fine radiant weapon, but outclassed by the Halberd of Vigilance and Drakethroat — mainly for a radiating-orb paladin.

Pike.why: The non-magical pike has arguably the best weapon-action combo in the game (Rush Attack + Brace + Piercing Strike), so it's one of the best base weapons to use while you're still on non-magical gear.

Spear.why: A simple, versatile, thrown weapon (highest-damage simple weapon two-handed, fire-dippable); has some niche use as a thrown weapon, but no one starts with one and hand axes/javelins are better throws — mostly overshadowed.

Jagged Spear.why: A no-enhancement spear applying Tortured (disadvantage on con saves) on a DC10 Charisma save; the condition is weak and unreliable, but it can debuff bleed-immune enemies — niche for a reverb + con-save build.

Light of Creation.why: A +1 halberd with +1d6 lightning, but you must pass a DC10 con save each attack or be stunned (unless you're a construct); only usable by a character that reliably passes the save, and it's outclassed before the construct combo comes online.

Harmonium Halberd.why: A +1 halberd granting +2 Strength (max 23) but -1 Int/Wis (hurting Wisdom saves); competes with Strength elixirs (which go higher, free, no weapon slot), so only a natural-strength character wants it, and even then it's unexciting.

Glaive.why: Non-magical two-handed reach d10 with Rush Attack, Lacerate, and Brace — fine, but overshadowed by the better weapon actions on the non-magical pike, so it sits in D.

Halberd.why: Identical to the glaive but trades Brace for Cleave, which only beats a normal attack when hitting 3 enemies — so as a non-magical weapon it's slightly worse than the glaive.

+1 Glaive.why: A generic +1 glaive; the strictly-superior Returning Pike is sold by the same early merchants, so there's no reason to buy it.

+1 Halberd.why: A generic +1 halberd; like the +1 glaive, it's outclassed by the Returning Pike available at the same early merchants.

+1 Pike.why: A generic +1 pike; the Returning Pike is a strictly-better +1 pike available from the same early merchant, so the plain version is pointless.

Merregon Halberd.why: Just a +1 halberd with a cool icon and no special effect — identical to a weapon you could already have, so there's no reason to use it.

Spear of Night.why: A story-event +1 spear with no other magical effect — only rated for its weapon use, which at that point is bottom-tier.

+2 Spear.why: A generic +2 spear found only at the very end; you'll have far better spears from Act 2 story events, so don't bother.

Breaching Pikestaff.why: A +2 pike with +1d4 force and no other effect; in Act 3 it's worse than the Halberd of Vigilance or a Drakethroat-enchanted weapon — nothing exciting.

Infernal Spear.why: A +1 spear with a flat 3 poison that (unlike the Hellbeard) doesn't poison on every hit; by Act 3 you have far better spears, and even poison builds have better options.

Magical Pike.why: Just a renamed +1 pike — a +1 weapon has no use in Act 3.
